About

A GP at the forefront
of cannabis medicine

Executive Committee

An independent, society of over 500 UK clinicians that produces national prescribing guidance and good-practice standards, delivers CPD-accredited training, and engages with policymakers and police. The Society has developed guidance with roads policing leaders on driving and medical cannabis use. As an Executive Committee member, Richard contributes to clinician education and the development of clinical guidance.

Clinical Training

Broad foundations

Clinical rotations across neurology, psychiatry, general practice, paediatric surgery, and care of the elderly, alongside work in neurodevelopmental assessment clinics, acute care, and with unaccompanied asylum-seeker children.

Mental Health

Early Intervention in Psychosis

EIP services aim to recognise psychosis at its earliest stage, often during the prodromal period of subtle decline and social withdrawal before a first episode. By acting early and providing close, sustained follow-up, family involvement, and combined psychological and medical care, they can change the course of the illness and give people a much better chance of recovery than standard psychiatric care. Richard worked as a care coordinator in an EIP service, supporting young people through a first episode of psychosis using CBT and trauma-informed practice.

I am a UK-based GP with a portfolio career spanning primary care, out-of-hours services, and specialist medical cannabis practice. My academic background reflects an interest in both conventional and plant-based medicine: a BSc (Hons) in Herbal Medicine, a postgraduate diploma in Mental Health Practice, and my medical degree (MBChB). The herbal medicine training was a rigorous, clinically grounded, and evidence-based programme encompassing quality control, research methodology, and supervised practice, including time in NHS Dermatology at Whipps Cross Hospital.

My broader clinical background includes care coordination within an Early Intervention in Psychosis service, working with 18 to 36 year olds experiencing their first episode of psychosis. That grounding in mental health, alongside training in CBT and trauma-informed approaches, shapes how I think about the patients I see in cannabis medicine, many of whom carry significant complexity.

In medical cannabis practice I have delivered over 20,000 consultations, with particular depth in neurodevelopmental disorders, chronic pain, anxiety, sleep conditions, and complex high-utilisation patients. I work within the current evidence base for efficacy and risk minimisation. Alongside clinical work I train and mentor GPs and pharmacist prescribers entering this field, and contribute regularly to multidisciplinary team discussions on complex cases.

This site is where I write about the research I find compelling, the clinical questions I keep encountering, and the conversations that matter most in cannabis medicine right now. The site itself is new, but many of the articles here draw on reflections I have been collecting and anonymising over several years of practice. Writing has always been how I process the clinical complexity and emotional weight of caring for vulnerable patients every day, and gathering those notes in one place felt like a natural next step. If you are a clinician, researcher, journalist, or organisation working in this space, I am glad to connect.

Writing

The content on this site is for educational and informational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional regarding any medical condition or treatment.

Comments & discussion

I welcome discussion on everything I write here. Medicine is rarely black and white, and botanical medicine shows even greater variance than most fields. I am genuinely open to other opinions and perspectives, and I read every comment.

Comments are approved before they appear, so there may be a short delay. Frank discussion of drugs, drug use, and differing clinical views is welcome. Hateful or abusive comments are not.

All articles

New article

Paste or type your article. Each paragraph is a new line. Start a line with ## to make a section heading — e.g. ## My heading

Publish to your website

Your edits are saved in this browser tab. Download the file below and upload it to GitHub to make them permanent and live.

How to publish on GitHub

  1. Download the file above — it will be called hazletthealth.html
  2. Go to your GitHub repository in your browser
  3. Click on index.html in the file list
  4. Click the pencil icon (Edit) at the top right
  5. Click the three-dot menu ⋯ then Delete file and commit the deletion
  6. Back in the repository, click Add file → Upload files
  7. Drag in your downloaded hazletthealth.html file
  8. Before committing, rename it to index.html in the filename box
  9. Click Commit changes — your site updates within about a minute

Tip: once you are comfortable with GitHub, there is a faster drag-and-drop method. Ask Dr Claude to explain it when you are ready.
